answer: Comte’s sharpest point is in `charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99`, chapter 1: the formal institutions of ancient slavery may be gone, but their doctrines remain alive in legal and moral teaching and are still brought into courts. On that basis, Manu chapter 8 in `unknown-manusmrti-347b76` does look like the kind of material Comte was warning about, with one qualification: Manu is not itself a downstream survival of slave law, but an example of the slave-normalized jurisprudence later traditions might inherit. In chapter 8, slaves are folded into legal procedure and status rules with no reflective pause: a slave can serve as fallback witness when no better witness is available (Mn_8.70); a slave is grouped with wife and son as someone who may be beaten for fault (Mn_8.299); slavery is classified into legal origins (Mn_8.415); and a slave is said to have no property of his own (Mn_8.416). So the answer is yes in substance: Manu chapter 8 exemplifies the normalization of slave status inside ordinary law that Comte says can continue to shape later legal categories.
